coredata.dc:description: According to the latest report by International Agency for Research on Cancer, 19.3 million new cancer cases and 10 million cancer deaths were globally reported in 2020. Early diagnosis can reduce these numbers significantly, and biosensors have appeared to be a solution to this problem as, unlike the traditional methods, they have low cost, rapid process, and do not need experts present on site for use. These devices have been incorporated to detect many cancer biomarkers and measure cancer drug delivery. To design these biosensors, a researcher must know about their different types, properties of nanomaterials, and cancer biomarkers. Among all types of biosensors, electrochemical and optical biosensors are the most sensitive and promising sensors for detecting complicated diseases like cancer. The carbon-based nanomaterial family has attracted lots of attention due to their low cost, easy preparation, biocompatibility, and significant electrochemical and optical properties. In this review, we have discussed the application of graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon dots (CDs), and fullerene (C60), for designing different electrochemical and optical cancer-detecting biosensors. Furthermore, the application of these carbon-based biosensors for detecting seven widely studied cancer biomarkers (HER2, CEA, CA125, VEGF, PSA, Alpha-fetoprotein, and miRNA21) is reviewed. Finally, various fabricated carbon-based biosensors for detecting cancer biomarkers and anticancer drugs are comprehensively summarized as well.

head.ce:title: Carbon-based biosensors from graphene family to carbon dots: A viewpoint in cancer detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: According to the latest report by International Agency for Research on Cancer, 19.3 million new cancer cases and 10 million cancer deaths were globally reported in 2020. Early diagnosis can reduce these numbers significantly, and biosensors have appeared to be a solution to this problem as, unlike the traditional methods, they have low cost, rapid process, and do not need experts present on site for use. These devices have been incorporated to detect many cancer biomarkers and measure cancer drug delivery. To design these biosensors, a researcher must know about their different types, properties of nanomaterials, and cancer biomarkers. Among all types of biosensors, electrochemical and optical biosensors are the most sensitive and promising sensors for detecting complicated diseases like cancer. The carbon-based nanomaterial family has attracted lots of attention due to their low cost, easy preparation, biocompatibility, and significant electrochemical and optical properties. In this review, we have discussed the application of graphene and its derivatives, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon dots (CDs), and fullerene (C60), for designing different electrochemical and optical cancer-detecting biosensors. Furthermore, the application of these carbon-based biosensors for detecting seven widely studied cancer biomarkers (HER2, CEA, CA125, VEGF, PSA, Alpha-fetoprotein, and miRNA21) is reviewed. Finally, various fabricated carbon-based biosensors for detecting cancer biomarkers and anticancer drugs are comprehensively summarized as well.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is the second leading cause of death and a severe health problem worldwide. Globally, 19.3 million new cancer cases and 10.0 million cancer-related deaths were reported in 2020. Estimates show that by 2040, the number of cases will rise 47% and reaches 28.4 million, most of which will happen in countries in transition. In countries with a high Human Development Index (HDI), the mortality/incidence rate ratio is much lower than in countries with lower HDIs due to easy access to early diagnosis and adequate treatment [1].

Accordingly, early cancer diagnosis methods are essential to minimize mortality rates, one of which is biosensors. Biosensors detect specific types of molecules with biological functionalities and can indicate different diseases [2]. These molecules are called biomarkers. Nucleic acids (for instance, non-coding RNA and micro RNA), peptides, antibodies, proteins (for instance, enzymes or receptors), and even a collection of alterations like the proteomic, metabolic signature, and gene expression are different biomarker types. These materials can be detected serially and non-invasively in circulation (plasma, whole blood, or serum), secretions (urine, stool, sputum, or nipple discharge), or excretions [3]. Generally, detecting cancer biomarkers can be classified into five groups: 1-diagnostic: assessing the absence or presence of cancer, 2-early detection, 3-prognostic: survival probabilities, aggressive phenotype, and behavior of cancer, 4-target: identifies the target molecules and their affection during cancer therapy, and 5-predictive: predicts the effectiveness of drugs and treatment [4].

Investigating reports in this field show that nearly 80% of all data comprise electrochemical and optical biosensors, showing their significance and potential [5]. Electrochemical biosensors measure four different types of signals; current (for amperometric and voltammetric biosensors), impedance, potential, and conductance. These signals result from the interaction of a biomarker and biorecognition molecules [6]. Optical biosensors measure the emitted, reflected, or absorbed photons from target molecules and provide multiplexed recognition (Fig. 1 -A) [7]. The most studied optical biosensors are surface plasmon resonance (SPR) (Fig. 1-B), fluorescence (Fig. 1-C), colorimetric, and Raman scattering (Fig. 1-D) [8] (see Fig. 2).

Nevertheless, the performance of biosensors commonly needs enhancement, and one of the best ways to improve it is by incorporating nanomaterials in their composition. Various nanomaterial types such as inorganic-based nanomaterials like metals, metal oxides, layered transition-metal disulfides or dioxides, quantum dots, silica, magnetic nanoparticles (NPs), and carbon nanomaterial family have been used to improve the performance of biosensors [13,14]. Carbon nanomaterials are the most important among all these materials because of the facility for surface functionalization, rapid electron transfer, biocompatibility, and electrochemical stability [15]. Graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon dots (CDs), and fullerenes are the most known members of this family. They have been successfully used in biosensors and many other biomedical applications [16].

This article first reviews the features and properties of the five most critical carbon-based materials and their applications for designing optical and electrochemical biosensors. Next, we discuss the carbon-based biosensors for detecting the seven most widely studied cancer biomarkers, including HER2, CEA, CA125, PSA, VEGF, Alpha-fetoprotein, and miRNA21. Finally, the shortcoming and prospects for designing better biosensors are discussed.

Nano-sized carbons have unique properties and are used in various fields. Almost all of the carbon nanomaterials mentioned in this article have an sp2 carbon network with a hexagonal structure, which is the cause of superior properties like high thermal and electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, and carrier mobility [17]. These carbon-based nanomaterials possess large surface areas, which facilitate enhancing the loading capacity and the mass transport of reactants for obtaining favorable and improved performance in terms of sensitivity [18]. These materials provide a system that can be very versatile, facilitating the incorporation of a wide range of probing mechanisms that can be adhered to the surface of the designed biosensors by covalent surface chemistry methods. Besides, their application can be improved even further by combining them with other processes and different materials, which makes designing and producing patient-friendly platforms [19].
In this section, the application of carbon-based biosensors for detecting and treatment of cancers is reviewed.

Graphene is a one-atom-thick, versatile allotrope of carbon that can be easily produced from graphite. It has a honeycomb structure with a two-dimensional layer of carbon atoms with sp2 hybridization and advantageous properties such as a high surface-to-volume ratio, ease of surface functionalization, high mechanical strength, and high thermal and electrical conductivity [20].

Polarization-dependent effects and tunable and broadband absorption are the optical properties of graphene that have been successfully used in spatial light sensors, SPR sensors, and optical fiber SPR sensors. These graphene-based sensors can be used to detect anticancer drugs and cell lines as well as single cells [21]. SPR is an optical technique that exploits the generation of electromagnetic waves (plasmons), and SPR sensors are the most common optical sensors and have been vastly investigated [22]. Fast detection, high-quality data analysis, high sensitivity, and wide application are the features of these sensors [23]. In SPR sensors, however, where the sensor's sensitivity commonly deteriorates due to processes that affect the surface properties of the metallic layer, graphene can act as a protecting layer. Therefore, a broader range of metals can be used in SPR sensors, increasing the sensitivity by 3–4 orders [24]. Depositing graphene on the metallic film of SPR sensors also improves bimolecular absorption, enhances the electric field, and reduces costs [25]. For instance, Sun et al. designed an SPR sensor and used graphene and barium titanate (BaTiO3) together in its structure. They aimed to reach an optimum combination of silver, BaTiO3, and graphene layers. They found that increasing the number of graphene layers while keeping the thickness of BaTiO3 and silver constant improves the sensor's performance [26], or in 2023, Salehnezhad et al. prepared a Kretschmann structure with a graphene-MoS2 hybrid layer and suggested that the sensitivity of the sensor could be increased using a silicon layer due to the strong light-matter interaction between graphene and semiconductor layer [27].

It should be noted that graphene's optical absorption is only 2.3%, limiting its application in optical sensors; therefore, simple, developing inexpensive methods that enhance the light absorption of graphene is necessary [28]. Despite these poor optical properties, graphene has a broad window of other potentials, such as low charge transfer resistance and activity in different types of oxidation-reduction systems [29]. Because of these properties, graphene is a suitable material for constructing electrochemical biosensors. Graphene-based electrochemical biosensors are highly stable, respond quickly, and possess high sensitivity [30]. They have been used in food applications to detect tryptamine and erythromycin, in environmental applications to detect pesticides and ions, and in human health to detect dopamine, cholesterol, uric acid, pathogens, and cancers [31,32]. Their high agglomeration tendency, the lack of surface functionality, and low water solubility limit the direct application of graphene [33]. Hence they are used in composite form or are chemically modified.

Graphene can be functionalized with materials like metal oxides, polymer films, metal nanoparticles, quantum dots, and ionic liquids. Manganese dioxide (MnO2) is an excellent example in this regard due to its specific properties for designing biosensors. In graphene/MnO2 composites, graphene nanoparticles have better dispersion, showing better conductivity (due to the presence of the O atom in the structure of MnO2 with low conductivity) [34]. Gao et al. prepared poly-l-lysine (PLL)-functionalized graphene field-effect transistor biosensors by functionalization of PLL on the channel surface of the biosensor to immobilize DNA probes by the electrostatic force. Their results showed that using 2 μL of human serum samples, an miRNA detection range of five orders could be achieved for these biosensors that could be used for rapid diagnosis and screening of breast cancer [35].

Wang et al. proposed an aptamer-based biosensor by combining paper-based microfluidic and electrochemical detection methods. They fabricated a nanocomposite of amino-functionalized graphene/thione/gold particles for the working electrode. The surface area was increased by adding thionine and forming new Π-Π stacking interactions between modified graphene and thionine. Gold particles provided an immobilizing surface for anti-EGFR aptamer and generated electrochemical signals. The features of amino-functionalized graphene prevented the gold particles from agglomeration, which improved the sensor's sensitivity. This low-cost production aptasensor could be adopted in other environmental and clinical applications [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: Since graphene has diverse structures and can be modified into different materials, it is not known as an individual material but rather a vast group of materials [37]. The most highlighted group members are graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The Hummers, Staudenmaier, and Hofmann methods are the different routes of producing GO [38]. The oxygen functional groups of GO (carboxyl, hydroxyl, and active epoxy groups) make it more compatible with the immune system and make graphene water-soluble. This hydrophilic feature enables it to integrate with a broad range of inorganic materials and can be easily exfoliated [32,39]. rGO, however, is produced by reducing GO through electrochemical reduction, chemical reduction, and thermal reduction [40].

GO can quench photoluminescence; hence, it has been vastly used in optical biosensors. The carbon/oxygen ratio modulates this property, and as the ratio increases, this property improves [41]. The said ratio can be controlled by the reduction method and by decreasing the C–O bonds and increasing the C–C bonds. Fluorescent GO-based biosensors can detect molecules like protein, DNA, RNA, and metal ions [42]. GO has two prominent roles in these biosensors: 1-one or two DNA probes are bound on the surface of the GO via hydrogen binding or Π-Π stacking, and 2-GO quenches the emitted fluorescence dye [43].

In addition, GO can be modified using different materials to obtain desired properties for the final application. For instance, modifying GO with a carboxylic group (-COOH) increases its surface resonance propagation and electrical properties. This modified material can improve the biological affinity and sensitivity of SPR sensors. Chiu et al. coated the surface of an Au film to enhance its energy propagation and represented a sensitive SPR chip for detecting cytokeratin 19 (CK19) protein, a biomarker for lung cancer. This immune sensor was successfully tested in human plasma. Compared to conventional SPR chips, this sensor had a better detection limit and shorter response time [44].

Incorporating a combination of GO and Fe3O4 in constructing a molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP)-based sensor can improve the performance of these biosensors. GO can enhance the surface area and analyte absorption, and Fe3O4 improves reusability and mass transfer rate. This method has been used in detecting p53 (a tumor biomarker) and capecitabine (an anticancer drug) in different biological samples [45,46]. In another study, GO was used in combination with other materials for detecting human papillomavirus-16 (HPV-16), which is the virus responsible for cervical cancer, by modification of an indium-tin oxide-coated glass electrode using GO and silver-coated gold nanoparticles, followed by immobilization of HPV-16 specific DNA probes on the modified surface, which could be used for diagnosis of HPV-16 infection in its early stage [47].

The presence of oxygen reduces conductivity, so in electrochemical biosensors, GO is used in the form of a hybrid matrix with conductive or semi-conductive material [48]. A controlled reduction process can balance the conductivity and presence of oxygen too. The redox process creates structural defects in the material and increases the number of edge atoms. Due to its unique structure, rGO-based electrochemical biosensors are more sensitive than graphene-based types [49]. Composites containing rGO and conducting polymers (CPs) or metals have attracted much attention recently. In rGO/CP composites, electrochemical or chemical polymerization of monomers happens in the presence of rGO, and a system with vast numbers of Π-Π conjugated electrons is formed. In the case of using GO, the post-reduction method is used [50].

Furthermore, rGO nanocomposites containing metal nanoparticles like Au, Ag, Ce, and Ti are very important to open new windows for both groups of materials in various applications such as biosensors, where synergism effect is observed for the specific properties of each material and hybrid materials with exceptional performances are obtained [51]. Cerium oxide (CeO2) is ideal for point-of-care devices because of its biocompatibility, electrical conductivity, large surface area, and high oxygen transferability [52]. CeO2 nanoparticles have a high tendency to aggregate, which affects their properties. Using a supporting matrix-like rGO can improve the dispersion and conductivity of CeO2 nanoparticles. For instance, Pachauri and coworkers synthesized the composite via in situ reductions of CeO2-GO solution and used it as a coating of an electrode surface for oral cancer detection. The fabricated sensor retained its selectivity in the presence of interleukin 8, mucin 16, sodium chloride, and glucose [53]. Shahrokhian and Salimian fabricated a polymer-modified rGO-based biosensor for detecting breast cancer. A fiber-like structure of poly-(pyrrole-3-carboxylic acid) was deposited on the edge and surface of the rGO electrode via electrode polymerization of its monomer. The conductive polymer provides more active sites for immobilizing DNA on the surface of the electrode, and rGO increases the surface area and electron transfer rate. This sensor showed significant reusability, selectivity, and low detection limit and is a new paradigm in designing biosensors [54].

GQDs have a relatively short fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield, which hampers their utilization in optical fields. On the other hand, their size is a barrier to conductivity, and they are counted as semiconductors or even insulators and cannot be used in electrochemical applications without modification. There are two ways for modifying GQDs: 1-doping heteroatoms and 2- surface and edge functionalization. Nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), chlorine (Cl), boron (B), silicon (Si), fluorine (F), selenium (Se), and phosphorus (P) are examples of heteroatoms that can be doped either during the synthesize of GQDs or after it. In the second method, the edge and surface of GQDs are functionalized with amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, or any other electron-accepting/donating groups. In addition, polymers like polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethylene imine (PEI) are candidates for modifying GQDs and can increase the binding sites for biomolecule immobilization on the surface of GQDs [58].

Fluorescent sensors based on non-radiative energy transfer have gained lots of attention in medical fields and clinical applications because of their sensitivity, easy operation, and real-time detection [59]. However, improving these sensors' biocompatibility and optical properties has been highly demanded. In this context, Kalkal et al. fabricated a new sensor by incorporating amine-functionalized and nitrogen-doped GQDs (amine-N-GQDs) as an energy donor and Au nanoparticles as an energy acceptor. Here, amine-N-GQDs have a long fluorescence lifetime and high quantum yield and improve the sensor's detection limit and performance in recognizing lung cancer [60]. In a recent study, donor-acceptor energy transfer-based strategies were used to prepare nanoparticles in which hydrophilic tertiary amine-grafted polythiophene played the role of the donor and blue fluorescent graphene quantum dots played the role of the accepter and worked as pH-sensitive detection of cancer cells. Broad-range, tunable GQD-loading contents and rapid acid-triggered fluorescent behavior under acidic pH could be obtained using this system, which could be utilized for the rapid detection of cancer cells [61].

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are one-dimensional rolled graphene sheets with an approximate diameter of 1 nm and 1–100 nm in length. After graphene, CNTs are the most highlighted allotrope of carbon for designing biosensors due to their unique properties. They have a high surface-to-volume ratio, excellent mechanical properties, 104–107 S/m electrical conductivity, and suitable optical properties. The high surface-to-volume ratio makes the CNT-based biosensors ultra-sensitive for detecting biological species at very low concentrations. The high conductivity and electron transfer rate results in a fast response time and the advanced mechanical properties of these materials make the sensor highly stable with a long life span. Generally, CNTs have two types, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). While MWCNTs have a larger size and higher conductivity, they form larger aggregates (directly related to toxicity) and are unsuitable for in-vivo detections. SWCNTs, however, exhibit remarkable optical properties [62]. In Fig. 2 (D-E), MWCNT with three different chiralities and the effect of the rolling method on the final type of CNT are shown. SWCNTs can quench fluorophores, have photoluminescence properties, and have been used in optical biosensors [63]. For instance, Williams and coworkers designed an optical sensor to detect ‌‌‌‌prostate cancer early on by sensing urokinase plasminogen activator (uPA), which is higher in cancer patients than in healthy patients. They used the optical properties of SWCNTs to detect uPA via a modulated band gap in antibody conjunction and tested the process in plasma, whole blood, and serum [64].

Due to the favorable electrical properties of CNTs, they have also been used to prepare electrochemical biosensors. For instance, Park et al. produced nanocomposites of CNT and polyaniline for detecting VEGF165 as a tumor marker, which showed good stability in biological conditions, selectivity, and reproducibility after several measurement times [65]. In 2023, Meng et al. prepared flexible electrochemical sensors to detect H2O2, which is released by HepG2, based on rGO and CNT flexible hybrid films and then anchored MnO2 nanoflowers and Co nanospheres on rGO-CNT by electrodeposition. In this system, an expanded detection range, high sensitivity, low detection limit, excellent repeatability, reproducibility, stability, and selectivity was observed, which could pave the way for using flexible substrate material and transition metals as well as transition metal compounds to develop flexible electrodes for cancer detection [66].

Carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon materials with an average size of less than 20 nm. These materials have high stability, good biocompatibility, high quantum yield, and high thermal stability. The properties of these materials are highly related to the synthesizing method, which creates functional groups on the surface. CDs are used in various fields like energy application, bioimaging, phototherapy, drug delivery, and sensors [67]. These materials’ electron donor/acceptor ability and low-cost production have resulted in booming research for designing CD-based optical biosensors [68]. The strategies for developing such a sensor are: 1-there must be a change in the fluorescence signal after the interaction of CD and target molecule; 2- generating sensing ability by conjugation of a receptor or functional group, by post-modification; 3-fluorophores, quencher, and substrate integration of these carbon-based materials as sensory [69]. In the case of electrochemical biosensors, these materials are used to increase the surface area on the electrode to improve the immobilization of recognition elements. Furthermore, CDs can act as reducing agents and stabilizers for fabricating nano-metallic composites-based electrochemical biosensors [70].

Zarei-Ghobadi and coworkers designed an optical biosensor to detect the genome of human T-lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), a retrovirus that causes human cancer body. They used the photoluminescence properties of CDs in the presence of Fe3O4 caped Au (Fe3O4@Au). The probed CDs absorb on the surface of Fe3O4@Au, which quenches the fluorescence emitting of CDs. By adding the targeted virus into the system, the CDs are desorbed, and the fluorescence emitting recovers, which is known as a detection signal. One of the defects of this sensor was the presence of other viruses like HIV and hepatitis B, and the sensitivity was not significant [71].

Because of their large surface area, CDs have considerable electrocatalytic activity, but oxygen groups on the surface reduce the conductivity; therefore, in electrochemical biosensors, CDs are combined with conductive materials. Bai et al. prepared the composition of CDs, MWCNTs, and glassy carbon electrodes for detecting H2O2, a signaling molecule to numerous diseases, including cancers. Here MWCNT is a matrix for CDs and acts as an electron acceptor in the system, and CDs are the doped catalyst and electron donor particles. H2O2 was detected through a reduction process, and the result showed that CDs/MWCNTs have better performance than MWCNTs or CDs alone. Overloading CDs in this system reduces the electrical conductivity, and the system with few CDs does not have enough active sites [72].

Fullerenes have unique symmetric cage structures with numerous sizes (C60, C80, C20, and so forth). C60 is the most abundant type of fullerene, with 60 carbon atoms in its design and 7.0 × 10−10 diameter. The zero-dimensional C60 has high thermal stability (up to 1000 C), a long-living triple state, and nucleophilic and electrophilic characteristics [73]. In Fig. 2 (F-g), the schematic strucutre of fullerene C60 and fullerene C70 are shown. C60 can accommodate endohedral metal atoms and multiple joint electrochemical and optical properties of carbon nanomaterials [74]. Individual C60 is ineffective in constructing biosensors, and they usually act as a mediator to conjugate with biomolecules and promote electron transfer in electrodes. However, they have to be functionalized first. The functionalization can occur on the exterior of the carbon cage or inside it, and it varies based on the nature of the analyte and biosensor type [75].

Han and coworkers designed an electrochemical aptasensor for detecting cancer-related proteins. They used gold nanoparticles and onion-like mesoporous graphene sheets (O-GS) as a probe for glassy carbon electrodes and immobilized aptamers on them [57]. On the other hand, they designed a tracing tag by modifying C60. For developing the tracer, 10-perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride and l-cysteine were used to functionalize C60 nanoparticles (FC60 NPs). This functionalization results in water solubility and increasing active sites. Then FC60NPs were decorated with Prussian blue carried gold nanoparticles. This process increases amperometric response and electrocatalytic activity. Finally, aptamers were immobilized on their surface. This sandwich-like aptasensor could successfully detect platelet-derived growth factor B-chain, a cancer-related protein, with a limit of detection of 0.6 pM in the range of 0.002– 40 nM [57].

As we mentioned in the manuscript, various types of molecular biomarkers can be selected as a target in molecular detection via liquid biopsy techniques. Among all these biomarkers, some have been vastly studied and gained researchers’ curiosity for designing fast and reliable biosensors. According to recent studies, although many of these biomarkers are commercially well-known in clinical cancer diagnostics, many recent studies still focus on their importance as accurate and sensitive markers in the screening and prognosis of different kinds of cancers.

In the following sections, these famous and commercially known biomarkers and instances of their application are explained due to their feasibility and accessibility for researchers for chip-based detection on the lab scale. These biomarkers can be considered the most reliable choices for molecular research or science-based businesses for launching new types of patient- and healthcare-user-friendly diagnostics or screening platforms.

Human epidermal growth factor 2 (HER2) is a ligand-free membrane receptor tyrosine kinase located on chromosome-17 [76]. When HER2 is activated, it affects cell survival and proliferation. The standard dose of HER2 in the blood is 4– 14 ng/mL, while the concentration of this oncogene in breast cancer increases to 14–75 ng/mL [77]. Hence, HER2 is an important marker that should be considered in cancer diagnosis. It should be noted that HER2 is a versatile biomarker, and disorders in HER2 can be used for prediction and prognosis in biomarkers in various tumor types and there have been reports on the efficiency of this oncogene in different types of cancers like bladder, colorectal, non-small-cell-lung cancer, and biliary tract cancers [78,79]. The prominence of HER2 in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer makes it an ideal candidate for personalized cancer therapy and drug development. HER2 targeted therapy has one of the most successful markets with many FDA-approved drugs and many in R&D pipelines and clinical trials [80].

Most proposed sensors for detecting HER2 are designed to detect either HER2 protein or HER2-positive cancer cells. One of the most accurate selective electrochemical biosensors for detecting cancer-positive cells was proposed by Salahandish and her group for detecting SK-BR3 cancer cells that highly overexpress the HER2 gene. This work used a novel composition of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs)/rGO/polyaniline (PANI) to modify the electrode. NDA-MB231 and ZR-75-1 cancer cell lines with low and middle levels of HER2 expression were used to examine the sensor's sensitivity. The biosensor could successfully distinguish the different cancer cell lines by showing various intensity peaks. In the optimum condition, the sensor had a detection limit of 2 cells/mL in the linear range of 10 to 5 × 106 cells/mL. The use of AgNPs in this biosensor significantly reduced the cost of sensor preparation [81].

Another way to reduce production costs is using laser-scribed graphene (LSG), which results in fast electron mobility and high electrocatalytic activity. Rauf and coworkers used LSG as the basement of their electrode and modified it with Au NPs for detecting HER2 proteins. They could reach a morphology of the 3D Christmas tree structure of Au NPs by balancing the applied potential and concentration of HAuCl4 precursor in an electrochemical deposition. The developed biosensor in this study did not have a counter and reference electrodes and could detect HER2 on a smartphone-based platform in a linear range of 0.1–200 ng/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.008 ng/mL [82].

Gu et al. developed an aptasensor with the help of a ZrHf metal-organic framework (MOF) coupled with CDs to simultaneously determine HER2 and HER2 overexpressed MCF7 cancer cells. The crystalline structure of MOFs usually hinders the appropriate anchoring of the aptamers. Here, the addition of CDs decreases the crystalline structure, and because they contain –NH2 groups on their surface, the sensitivity of the biosensor has been significantly increased. The renewability of the sensor was tested for 15 runs and could detect HER2 with an LOD of 19 fg/mL in the range of 0.001–10 ng/mL and MCF7 cells with an LOD of 23 cells/mL in the range of 1 × 10 2 to 1 × 105 cell/mL [83].

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), an oncofetal antigen with 180–200 kDa molecular weight, is a valuable biomarker for detecting pancreatic, liver, breast, and gastric cancers. Measuring CEA is used for cancer detection, chemotherapy response evaluation, and recovery after surgery. The concentration of this biomarker in a healthy individual is below 5 ng/mL, while in most cancer cases, it is above 20 ng/mL [84].

As CEA is a significant biomarker for different cancers, there have been numerous reports of detecting this biomarker by multiple biosensors with innovative materials [85]. A new material recently used for designing biosensors is noble metal nano dendrites (NDs), which can provide great active sites for immobilizing recognition elements. The chemical composition of NDs can be tuned by combining them with other nanomaterials, mainly resulting in synergistic effects. Accordingly, Lv et al. designed rhodium@palladium ND-based biosensors and added sulfur-functionalized MWCNTs to the system to overcome the surface effects and aggregation of NDs. This composite showed good reproducibility and a broad linear range from 25 fg/mL to 100 ng/mL for CEA detection [86]. Zhang et al. incorporated another type of bimetallic NDs for designing a sandwich-type electrochemical biosensor for detecting CEA. In this work, polydopamine (PDA) functionalized graphene was used as the supporting matrix for the immobilization of Pd–Pt NDs and provided a large surface area for immobilization of secondary aptamer (first aptamers were immobilized on a GCE electrode modified with PDA@Gr). This biosensor could detect the analyte in the linear range of 50 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL with an LOD of 6.3 pg/mL. After two weeks of storage, the sensor showed desirable stability and only lost 3.3% of its detection value [87].

The above-mentioned electrochemical biosensors are based on the turn-off method in which the electrochemical activities decrease after the absorption of the analyte. Li et al. designed a sandwich-type electrochemical aptasensor based on the signal-on method. In the presence of the target, the produced signal by these sensors increases; therefore, they can detect the analyte in the broader range and with lower LOD. The label is made by modification of CNT with poly (ferrocenyl glycidyl ether) and subsequent anchoring of secondary aptamer on its surface. During the detection process, CEA is absorbed on the gold electrode modified with aptamer then the label interacts with another part of the CEA. The tag contains an abundant amount of ferrocene derivatives and has high conductivity. Hence after the interaction, it amplifies the signal. The sensor can detect CEA with an LOD of‌ 2.8 × 10 −16 g/mL, which is almost 1000 times more sensitive than the previously-mentioned sensors [88].

Some of the designed optical biosensors for detecting CEA have appeared to be more sensitive than the electrochemical ones. Wang et al. designed a fluorescence – infrared absorption – mode nanoprobe for detecting CEA. They used the longitudinal and transverse optical properties of SiO2 to create the sensor, and for amplifying the signal, they added CDs to the system. The prepared biosensor showed one of the lowest detection limits with a recovery range (98.3–106.6%) and comparable performance with the ELISA kit (92–104%), and the platform has the potential for the detection of other cancer biomarkers [89].

Carbohydrate Antigen 125 (CA125) is a mucinous glycoprotein that has been one of the most valuable biomarkers in the screening and detecting ovarian cancer in the past four decades. Almost in 50% of early-stage tumors and 92% of advanced ovarian cancers, this biomarker has a concentration of more than 35 U/mL. Unfortunately, the utilization of CA125 in ovarian cancer has resulted in many false-positive reports. Therefore, measuring other biomarkers like human epididymis protein 4 is necessary for engaging more accurate reports [90].

One of the best choices for screening CA125 is using paper-based immunosensors. These sensors have good biocompatibility, low cost, easy availability, and disposability. Fan et al. designed a paper-based electrochemical biosensor modified with rGO/thionine/AuNPs composite. Thionine and rGo were attached through Π-Π stacking interaction, and subsequently, the AuNPs are immobilized on the rGO/thionine composite through amino group interactions. Thionine is an electro-active material that induces redox peaks and elevates the sensitivity of the biosensor. This work showed improved performance compared to other reported works and had a relative error of
<
8.05% with the ELISA method [91]. Biswas and coworkers synthesized ZR-trimesic acid MOF (MOF-808) on the surface of the CNT and incorporated it in constructing an imunosensor to detect CA125. The results showed that the redox peak in the CV process for MOF-808/CNT-modified electrode is 1.5 times more than the CNT-modified electrode. This biosensor was able to detect CA125 with an LOD of 0.5 pg/mL in linear ranges of 0.1–30 and 0.001–0.1 ng/mL [92].

Prostate cancer is the second most common cancer among men, with more than 1.2 million new cases and 350,000 related deaths. Prostate cancer is highly dependent on age; more than 85% of diagnosed patients were >60 years old [93]. The concentration of prostate-specific antigen in the blood is one factor that has been widely studied for detecting and treating prostate cancer. It is believed that the prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a liquefier for seminal fluid in men's bodies, and in normal conditions, only a minute level can leak into the blood. Reports show that healthcare centers with higher PSA screening rates had lower subsequent rates of metastatic prostate cancer, which demonstrates the value of this biomarker in the detection, screening, and prognosis of prostate cancer. The elevated PSA level alone is not a reliable source; therefore, more accurate detection markers are required [94,95].

Numerous reports have detected this analyte in serum, saliva, and blood samples [96]. In a work by Jia et al., 3D graphene aerogel was used to immobilize poly (3, 4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT), a conductive polymer, and AuNPs. PEDOT has remarkable physical and electrochemical properties, and the addition of graphene aerogel inhibits polymer aggregation and subsequent conductivity loss. The resulting composite has a notable conductive and large surface area for immobilizing biomolecules. This label-free biosensor detected PSA with an LOD of 0.03 pg/mL in a linear range of 0.0001– 50 ng/mL in the optimum conditions. After 14 days of storage, this biosensor maintained 86.56% of its initial value [97]. The biosensor's durability is directly related to the composite's stability. To this aim, Suresh and coworkers designed a sandwich-type electrochemical biosensor with copper nanoparticles (CuNPS), C60, hydroquinone, and primary antibody as a probe and horseradish peroxide/secondary antibody as a signal amplifier. This biosensor maintained 94.25% of its initial values after 25 days of storage. Further, the probe was successfully tested on two different electrodes (GCE and screen-printed paper) as proof of concept. This biosensor responded to PSA in a linear range of 0.005– 20 ng/mL with an LOD of 0.002 ng/mL [98].

Compared to electrochemical methods, the reported optical biosensors for detecting PSA are less sensitive and detect the analyte in more concentrated and broader ranges. One proposed method was Liu et al. who used the G peak shift rate graphene in the Raman approach. The peak shift through the binding of antigen and immobilized aptamer on the surface of the graphene. This biosensor detected the analyte in the range of 0.05–25 ng/mL [99]. A more comprehensive range (up to 100 ng/mL) is achievable by a hairpin-DNA labeled CD and GO in a FRET system. The labeled CDs absorb on the surface of the GO and result in the quenching of fluorescence properties. By adding the target molecules, due to the attachment of DNAs and PSA, the CDs would be released and recover their optical properties [100].

The formation of new blood vessels is a malignant phenotype in breast cancer and many other cancers. Vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is an angiogenetic regulator in normal conditions [101]. In fact, angiogenesis is an important factor in cancer development and growth [102]. In addition to angiogenesis, VEGF can affect the host response to the tumor by affecting the present immune cells in the tumor microenvironment. VEGF is a suitable target for cancer therapy to reduce cancer's burden and improve survival [103].

Sun et al. designed an origami paper-based biosensor modified with amino-functional SWCNT, methylene blue, and AuNPs. The origami concept has a much easier fabrication process than a regular paper-based biosensor. Because all the electrodes are printed on the same paper side, fewer printing masks will be used, reducing production costs. Moreover, the fabricated sensor maintained most of its performance for six weeks (85.23%), one of the most extended reported lifetimes for biosensors. The biosensor detected VEGF in a wide linear range of 0.01–100 ng/mL with an LOD of 10 pg/mL [104].

Cui et al. designed an optical biosensor to detect VEGF by porphyrin-based covalent organic frameworks (P–COF) and CDs. The aptamers were immobilized on the surface of the CDs, and P–COF strongly interacts with the CDs through its porous structure and residual amino groups on its surface. The absorption of CDs quenches the fluorescence properties, and adding the analyte recovers them. The sensor showed suitable biocompatibility and was also tested to detect living MCF-7 breast cancer cells by attaching the aptamers to the cells' nuclein [105].

Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a glycoprotein with an average weight of 70 kD and is known as one of the most studied biomarkers for liver cancer or hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC). The concentration of AFP is almost the same in all ages in the range of 5– 10 ng/mL and has been indicated as a cancer biomarker in a range above 400 ng/mL [106]. According to clinical reports, HCC patients can be divided into AFP-positive and AFP-negative [107], which shows the importance of this biomarker in liver cancer detection.

Sun et al. designed MIP based optical biosensor for detecting AFP in human serum. They used N-isopropyl acrylamide (NIPAM), a thermally responsive material interacting with AFP molecules through its hydroxyl groups, and 4-vinyl phenylboronic acid (VPBA) which is a pH-responsive material that interacts with the target through covalent bonds as monomers. The overall properties of these materials resulted in a fast response and a sensitive optical biosensor for the detection of AFP. The MIP could interestingly mimic the biological recognition elements due to its ability to form covalent and non-covalent interactions with the analyte. Furthermore, the biosensor showed high stability and, after 15 days, only lost 1% of its initial values. In the optimum conditions sensor could detect AFP in the linear range of 10– 100 ng/mL with an LOD of 0.474 ng/mL [108].

Wang and coworkers developed a new electrochemical biosensor to detect AFP by modifying rGO/AuNPs with Fe3O4 and toluidine blue (TB). TB is a redox probe, and Fe3O4 increases the redox peaks by more electrocatalytic performance. In a comparative experiment with the ELISA method, the sensor showed a relative range of errors from −4.9% to 4.0% in clinical samples [109].

MiRNAs are short non-coding RNA with a length of 15–27 nucleotides, and they play essential roles in human health and disease development. miRNA-21 is present in most cells, and its concentration differs from cell to cell. It can be detected from different bodily fluids like plasma, urine, peripheral blood, and the like. miRNA-21 can be used to predict 29 diseases, including more than 11 types of cancers [110]. The recognition element for almost all miRNAs is ssDNA. Usually, because of the extremely low concentrations in body samples, the designed electrochemical biosensors for detecting these biomarkers need sample preparations or labeling [111].

Enzyme-assisted labels are the most common types; however, there have been reports for other signal amplifiers like hairpin assembly, rolling circle amplification, hybridization chain reaction, and strand displacement amplification [112]. One of the label-free methods for simultaneously detecting three different miRNAs was reported by Pothipor et al. by a biosensor consisting of three-screen-printed carbon electrodes to detect miRNA-210, miRNA-21, and miRNA-155 simultaneously, which are biomarkers of breast cancer at the early stages. They modified the surface of the electrode with a composition of AuNPs/GQD/GO to enhance the signal. After immobilizing thiol-modified miRNAs probes on the surface of the electrode, the concentration of analytes was measured by square wave voltammetry. The intensity of the peaks decreases with increasing target concentration. This sensor detected miRNA-210, miRNA-21, and miRNA-155 with a detection limit of 0.28, 0.04, and 0.33 fM respectively in the linear range of 0.001– 1000 pM [113].

Shin Low and coworkers designed an electrochemical smartphone-based biosensor for detecting miRNA-21 in saliva. This system consists of a cell phone, a circuit board with a Bluetooth module, and a disposable screen-printed rGO/Au electrode. The ssDNA probe was immobilized on the electrode surface, and the hybridization with miRNA-21 decreased the peak current with an increase in miR-21 target concentration. The biosensor could detect the target from 1 × 10−4 M to 1 × 10−12 M and showed comparable performance with the electrochemical workstations [114].

Wang et al. designed an enzymatic self-powered biosensor for detecting miRNA-21 in serum. Self-powered biosensors (SPBs) are a new type of electrochemical biosensors in which the concept of galvanic cells and electrochemical biosensors have been merged for detecting analytes. In their work, a suspension of sulfur-selenium co-doped graphene and AuNPs were prepared as the basement of the anode and cathode. The anode was formed by incubating the above suspension in glucose oxidase solution, and then the material was dropped on the surface of a carbon paper (CP). The cathode was prepared through the same process, but the capture probe was immobilized on the electrode's surface instead of incubating in glucose oxidase. Potassium ferricyanide was entrapped on an ultra-thin carbon porous carbon shell/uNPs bonded to cruciform DNA to amplify the signal. In the presence of miRNA-21, it interacts with the capture probe on the cathode, and then the cruciform DNA on the signal amplifier immobilizes on the cathode by paring with capture DNA. The process releases the potassium ferricyanide, dramatically increasing the circuit voltage. This biosensor can detect miRNA-21 in the broad range of 0.5–10000 fM and LOD of 1.5 × 10−16 mol‌L−1 [115].

Recently, extracellular vesicles (EVs) appear to be specific markers for monitoring, diagnosing, and characterizing cancer diseases. EVs are early-stage cancer biomarkers containing valuable information about their originated cells [116]. In epithelial cancer cells, the EVs release rate is more than in healthy ones. For detecting epithelial EVs in peripheral blood, specific and sensitive analytical methods are needed. Ortega and his team developed a sandwich-type electrochemical paper-based immunosensor for detecting CD81 as housekeeping and claudin7 as an epithelial marker for early detection of breast cancer. Claudin7 has a high level in most breast cancer primary tissues, and CD81 is a marker for EVs used for normalization of Claudin7 expression and guarantees that its variation is related to epithelial EVs level. Two electrochemical cells were printed on the same side of a paper to detect these two biomarkers using GO and silver ink. Then the GO was electrochemically reduced, and the antibodies were immobilized on the surfaces of the electrodes. This biosensor operated much faster than ELISA and evaluated both Claudin7/CD81 ratios. Claudin7 was more accurate in detecting breast cancer than classical markers like CA15.3 and CEA [117].

Epithelial cell adhesion molecule (EpCAM) is a membrane protein on the surface of circulating tumor cells. This protein is overexpressed in different cancers, including gallbladder, colorectal, liver, and pancreatic [118]. Shi et al. reported an aptamer-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) assay for detecting EpCAM. They used PEGylated GQD as a donor molecule because of its strong fluorescence emission (compared to bare GQD) and MoS2 as a quencher. PEGylated GQD labeled EpCAM aptamer immobilized on the surface of the MoS2 via van der Waals forces and result in quenching fluorescence properties. During the detection process, because of the strong interaction between EpCAM and the aptamer, labeled GQD detached from the MoS2 surface, fluorescent intensity recovered and resulted in EpCAM recognition [119].

Recently detecting long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) in blood has gained much attention because they play a significant role in physiological processes and can be used for prognostic purposes and clinical diagnostics [120]. Mei et al. represented an ultrasensitive screen-printed electrochemical biosensor for detecting a special lncRNA of lung cancer (MALAT 1). Their sensor used Au nanocage (AuNC) coupled with amide-modified MWCNT (MWCNT-NH2). Here the amide group makes MWCNTs more compatible with Au nanocages, less cytotoxic, and more water-soluble. This sensor had a low detection limit (42.8 fM), and compared to PCR, which is a standard method for RNA measuring, it is simpler, faster, and produced at a low cost [121].

Heat shock proteins are a group of chaperon molecules known as inhibitors for cancer cell apoptosis; therefore, they are suitable biomarkers at the early stages of cancer. Demirbakan and Sezgintürk developed an electrochemical biosensor made from a glassy carbon electrode probed by C60 to measure heat shock protein 70 (HSP70). C60 was modified, by H2SO4, to increase the carboxyl groups on its surface, and then the anti-HSP70 was immobilized on it. In this biosensor, fullerene increased the surface conductivity and absorption of anti-HSP70 and enabled the sensor to respond to the picogram level of HSP-70 [122]. Electrochemical and optical biosensors for detecting other types of cancer biomarkers are summarized in Table 1 , and MIP-based biosensors for detecting anti-cancer drugs are summarized in Table 2 .

Carbon-based biosensors are promising devices for detecting cancer diseases. Generally, optical and electrochemical biosensors can be divided into two groups, labeled and label-free. The labeled biosensors have higher accuracy and can detect the analyte in a broader range with lower LODs. The electrochemical biosensors based on the turn-on method have rarely been reported because of the difficulties in design. However, CNTs and graphene have proven to be very promising in this field, and developing turn-on biosensors with the help of mentioned carbon nanomaterial is recommended.

The overexpression of one biomarker is not always a sign of cancer disease. Therefore, the development of multiplex-detecting carbon-based biosensors is necessary. A connected series of self-powered carbon-based biosensors is suitable for detecting multiple biomarker cancers. As self-powered biosensors can recognize a wide range of biomarkers from tumor markers to drugs, we believe this method can play a significant role in the effective detection and monitoring of cancers in the future.

Further research is also required for designing biosensors based on non-invasive sampling methods, particularly tear, and saliva, which are especially suitable for children and the elderly. In other words, making biosensors more user-friendly can have a revolutionary impact on commercializing these sensors and detecting cancers in their early stages. To this aim, coupling smartphone-based platforms with carbon-based nanomaterials can shape the future of cancer detection using biosensors outside laboratories.

This review reports the latest published studies in detecting cancer diseases using carbon-based biosensors. High conductivity, ease of surface modification, and chiral structure of carbon nanomaterials have improved the performance of electrochemical and optical biosensors and enabled them to detect cancer at its early stages.

Optical biosensors exploit the recovery or quenching in optical properties of nanomaterials, which happens due to the conjugation of recognition elements and analytes. The turn-on optical biosensors have higher sensitivity and therefore are a better choice for cancer detection. In the case of electrochemical biosensors, carbon is usually modified by hybridization with metal nanoparticles or synthesizing conductive polymers on their surface, which increase the surface area for the immobilization of recognition elements and improvement in conductivity. We chose seven common cancer biomarkers to clarify the effect of these methods on biosensors' sensitivity and reviewed their structure and sensitivity in the detection of the same biomarker. The capability of these sensors for detecting many other cancer biomarkers has also been summarized, and outlooks for the future of cancer detection methods based on carbon nanomaterials are discussed.
